ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific Earl Grey tea term exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000A covers tea and tea-based beverages; no Earl Grey-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 is the closest available code for tea as a substance; no Earl Grey-specific code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cold serving of Earl Grey tea is approximately 240 ml (240 g), equivalent to a regular cup or glass as typically served in a Singapore café or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, hot brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea (plain, no milk or sugar) used as the nutrient basis. Earl Grey is black tea flavoured with bergamot oil; the bergamot flavouring contributes negligible macronutrients. Values adapted to represent plain brewed Earl Grey tea served cold (no added sugar or milk assumed). Cold preparation does not materially alter the nutrient profile versus hot-brewed tea.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Plain Tea — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed convention for non-glycemic beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed tea contains negligible carbohydrates (approximately 0.3 g per 100 ml) and has no measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature classify plain tea as having an effectively zero glycemic index. Per Project Veritas convention for zero-GI beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate content per serving is below the threshold for a meaningful glycemic load calculation.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 ml); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain cold Earl Grey tea contains approximately 7 mg sodium per 240 ml serving and is an appropriate beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension, provided no sugar syrups or high-sodium additives are included.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content; no glycemic impact when served without added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain cold Earl Grey tea without added sugar or sweetened syrups contributes essentially no carbohydrates or calories and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, café versions may contain added sugar or condensed milk that would significantly raise glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol; contains flavonoids from black tea
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain cold Earl Grey tea is free of saturated fat and cholesterol, and the black tea base provides flavonoids (including theaflavins and thearubigins) that have been associated with modest cardiovascular benefits in observational studies; individuals should avoid versions with added cream or full-fat milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Earl Grey Tea (Cold) is a chilled version of the classic Earl Grey tea, a black tea flavoured with oil extracted from the rind of the bergamot orange (Citrus bergamia), a citrus fruit grown primarily in southern Italy. The tea is brewed from black tea leaves — most commonly a blend of Chinese or Indian black teas — and then infused or scented with bergamot oil, which imparts a distinctive floral, citrusy, and slightly perfumed aroma and flavour. When served cold, the brewed tea is chilled or poured over ice, making it a refreshing beverage particularly popular in Singapore's warm climate. In Singapore cafés and restaurants, cold Earl Grey tea may be served plain, lightly sweetened, or with milk, though the nutritional profile described here assumes a plain, unsweetened preparation. Nutritionally, plain cold Earl Grey tea is essentially a zero-calorie beverage: it contains approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml, negligible protein and fat, and only trace carbohydrates (approximately 0.3 g per 100 ml). It provides a small amount of potassium (approximately 37 mg per 100 ml) and trace minerals. The tea contains caffeine (approximately 20–40 mg per 240 ml serving) and polyphenolic antioxidants including theaflavins and thearubigins from the black tea base, as well as bergapten and other furanocoumarins from the bergamot flavouring. Clinically, plain unsweetened cold Earl Grey tea is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, as it contributes negligible sodium, sugar, saturated fat, and calories. Individuals on warfarin or certain medications should be aware that bergamot compounds may interact with drug metabolism pathways.
common_user_queries: Is Earl Grey tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in cold Earl Grey tea?
Is iced Earl Grey tea healthy?

confidence_reason: Earl Grey tea is a well-characterised beverage; nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed black tea (NDB 14355), which is the closest approved-source match. The bergamot flavouring contributes negligible macronutrients. Cold preparation does not materially alter the nutrient profile. Confidence is medium rather than high because no Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for Earl Grey tea exists, and the USDA entry is for plain hot-brewed black tea adapted to this cold-served variant.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea (plain, no milk or sugar), adapted for cold-served Earl Grey tea. Earl Grey is black tea with bergamot oil flavouring; the bergamot contributes negligible macronutrients. 2. protein_g is naturally zero for plain brewed tea as this beverage contains no protein-contributing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero as plain brewed tea contains no fat. sugar_g is confirmed zero for plain unsweetened preparation per USDA FoodData Central. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for brewed tea (fibre remains in the leaf solids, not the liquid). 3. energy_kcal of 1.0 kcal per 100 ml reflects the trace carbohydrate content of brewed black tea per USDA SR Legacy. 4. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for non-glycemic beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate per serving is below the threshold for a meaningful glycemic load. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0 as plain brewed tea contains no fat fractions; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. Potassium (37 mg/100 ml), magnesium (3 mg/100 ml), phosphorus (1 mg/100 ml), iron (0.02 mg/100 ml), riboflavin (0.01 mg/100 ml), and zinc (0.02 mg/100 ml) are reported from USDA SR Legacy brewed black tea. 7. Vitamins A, B1, B3, B6, B12, C, D, E, folate, selenium, calcium, and cholesterol are 0.0 as reported in USDA SR Legacy for plain brewed black tea. 8. This record assumes plain unsweetened preparation. Café versions with added sugar, condensed milk, or flavoured syrups will have substantially different nutritional profiles. 9. Caffeine content is approximately 20–40 mg per 240 ml serving (not tracked in per_100g schema). 10. The food is listed under Chinese cuisine in the GrabFood source data; however, Earl Grey tea is of British origin and is served internationally. Cuisine field updated to 'British / International' to reflect true origin. 11. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 used as closest parent for 'tea beverage'; no Earl Grey-specific FoodOn term exists. FoodEx2 AN000A used as closest parent for tea and tea-based beverages. SNOMED CT 227313005 used as closest parent for tea as a substance.

improvement_actions_taken: Set glycemic_index to 1 (positive sentinel) per zero-GI beverage convention instead of 0 or null.
Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g (naturally zero), fat_g (naturally zero), sugar_g (confirmed zero), and dietary_fibre_g (confirmed zero).
Documented all -1 sentinel values replaced with 0.0 where USDA reports confirmed zero; retained potassium, magnesium, phosphorus, iron, riboflavin, zinc as positive numeric values from USDA source.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' for all three ontology systems.
Added multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Singaporean cuisine context.
Updated cuisine field to 'British / International' to reflect true origin while preserving GrabFood source context in notes.
